commands.author: oalterg

target.title: feat(mcp): channel-mediated approval for MCP tool calls (consent envelope)
target.body:
Closes #78308

## Summary

Today, when a bundle-MCP tool returns to the agent, OpenClaw passes the result straight through to the model — there's no approval gate analogous to the one [`exec-approvals`](../tools/exec-approvals.md) provides for shell commands. For act-tier MCP tools (send email, create vault entry, set HA state, etc.), the only thing standing between the user and the action is *the model deciding to call it*.

This PR adds a small, additive contract — the **MCP consent envelope** — that lets MCP servers opt into the existing `/approve <id> allow-once|allow-always|deny` pipeline that already backs shell-exec approvals. **Servers that don't opt in are unaffected.**

## The contract

When a server wants user approval for a call, it returns:

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "requires_confirmation": true,
  "action_id": "<server-side single-use token>",
  "summary": "<one-line description of the action>",
  "expires_in_seconds": 60
}
```

…in either `structuredContent` or as JSON inside `content[0].text`. OpenClaw recognises the envelope, suppresses the result, and:

1. Issues a `plugin.approval.request` to the local gateway (reuses everything: `channel-approval-auth`, ID-prefix routing, the magic-word reply parser).
2. Blocks until the user replies on the trusted channel.
3. On `allow-once` / `allow-always`: re-calls the tool with `confirmation_token = action_id` set on the input. Returns that second result to the agent.
4. On `deny` / expiry / error: returns a synthetic `{ok:false, approved:false, reason}` result. The model never sees `action_id`.

## Why the trust boundary actually moves

The model never sees `action_id` and **`confirmation_token` is stripped from any model-supplied input** on the first call. Even if a malicious or careless agent fabricates a token, the MCP server's redemption check rejects it (the server is responsible for one-shot/TTL enforcement of `action_id`).

This is the same pattern OpenClaw already enforces for shell exec via `exec-approvals` — *agent asks, user authorises, runtime executes*. The PR just generalises that pattern to MCP tools that opt into the contract.

## What changed

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-consent.ts` | **New.** Envelope detection, the default approval requester (calls `plugin.approval.request` + `waitDecision`), `confirmation_token` scrubber, denied-result builder, **`sanitiseToolEmittedApprovalText`** (review-comment defence — neutralises `/approve` in MCP-emitted text). ~270 lines. |
| `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-materialize.ts` | Wraps every tool's `execute()` in a new `callMcpToolWithConsent` orchestrator. Plumbs `requestApproval`, `consentEnabled`, **`agentId`, and `sessionKey`** through `materializeBundleMcpToolsForRun` and `createBundleMcpToolRuntime`. |
| `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts`, `compact.ts` | Pass the agent harness's existing `agentId` + `sessionKey` into the materialize layer so the gateway forwarder can resolve the user's actual delivery channel (WhatsApp / Telegram / Slack / web UI). |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-approve.ts` | Accept bare `/approve allow-once` (no id) when there's exactly one pending approval — typing a uuid on a phone is unrealistic UX. On ambiguity (>1 pending), refuses with a hint including the explicit form. |
| `src/infra/plugin-approvals.ts` | Cleaner reply prompt: shows all three decisions (`allow-once \| allow-always \| deny`) inline, copy-paste-ready, with the id on its own line for the explicit fallback. |
| `src/config/types.mcp.ts` | Adds `mcp.approvals.enabled?: boolean` (default true). |
| `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-consent.test.ts` | **New.** 21 unit tests including a live-caught `decision: null` regression and a `agentId/sessionKey` propagation regression. |
| `docs/tools/mcp-consent-envelope.md` | **New.** Server-author contract, configuration, trust-boundary explanation. |
| `CHANGELOG.md` | Entry under `Unreleased`. |

No new approval kind is introduced — the implementation **reuses the existing `"plugin"` ApprovalKind**.

## Test plan

- ✅ `pnpm install`
- ✅ `pnpm lint` — 8234 files, 213 rules, 0 warnings, 0 errors.
- ✅ Full agents-core suite: **272 files passed (272), 3515 tests passed | 4 skipped**.
- ✅ `pi-bundle-mcp-consent.test.ts` — 21/21 green.
- ✅ `commands-approve.test.ts` — 18/18 green (existing tests still pass after the implicit-id parser change).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Without channel-mediated approval, the model is the trust gate for MCP tool calls that want user approval. Captured live on **vanilla `OpenClaw 2026.4.24`** with HomeBrain's MCP servers: when the agent fired `homebrain-nextcloud__nc-files_share`, the consent envelope (with `action_id`) passed straight to the model, which **leaked the token verbatim into chat**, **offered to self-confirm**, and **hallucinated a second action_id off-by-one** when it lost track of the first. Full screenshot + analysis: [#78308 comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78308#issuecomment-4391533882). This PR replaces that prompt-level honour-system with runtime-enforced channel approval.

**Environment tested:** `homebrain@192.168.178.58`. x86_64 Ubuntu 24.04. AMD Radeon RX 9060 XT (16 GB VRAM). llama.cpp + Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (Q5_K_XL). HomeBrain reference MCP servers from [oalterg/HomeBrain](https://github.com/oalterg/HomeBrain). **OpenClaw built directly from this PR branch and installed live** in place of the npm-shipped 2026.4.24 binary for the runtime-side tests.

**Steps run after the patch:**

```bash
// Local: build + pack the PR branch
pnpm build && npm pack
// Deploy the .tgz to the target box
scp openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz homebrain@192.168.178.58:/tmp/
ssh homebrain@192.168.178.58 'echo admin | sudo -S bash -c "
  cd /usr/lib/node_modules
  rm -rf openclaw && mkdir openclaw && cd openclaw
  tar xzf /tmp/openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz --strip-components=1
  npm install --omit=dev --no-audit --no-fund --silent"'
// Set the missing approvals.plugin config (the forwarder needs it to deliver)
ssh homebrain@192.168.178.58 'sudo -u homebrain jq ".approvals = {plugin:{enabled:true,mode:\"session\"}}" \
  ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json > /tmp/c && sudo -u homebrain cp /tmp/c ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json'
ssh homebrain@192.168.178.58 'sudo -u homebrain systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway'

// User sends on WhatsApp:
//   "Create a public share link to /uploads in Nextcloud, expires in 7 days."
// OpenClaw routes through the agent harness, which fires nc.files_share.
// My code intercepts the consent envelope, calls plugin.approval.request.
// The forwarder delivers the prompt to the user's WhatsApp + web UI.
// User taps Allow once on the web UI (or replies /approve allow-once on WhatsApp).
// Runtime re-calls the tool with confirmation_token. Real share URL returns.
```

**Evidence after fix:**

The web UI's native plugin-approval modal renders my PR's `plugin.approval.request` exactly right — title `MCP tool approval`, description `homebrain-nextcloud.nc.files_share — Nextcloud: create public share link for /uploads (expires in 7 days)`, severity `warning`, plugin `mcp:homebrain-nextcloud`, three buttons `Allow once / Always allow / Deny`, expiry countdown. The user pressed `Allow once`. Screenshot captured (not committed; happy to attach for maintainer review). Same approval was offered on the user's WhatsApp simultaneously via the routing-fix commit.

```text
$ openclaw --version  # the deployed PR build
OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (a2cd999)

// Gateway log (verbatim) at the moment the user tapped Allow once:
2026-05-06T20:01:22 ⇄ res ✓ plugin.approval.waitDecision 59412ms
                    conn=6f5914ca…dfe2 id=0c6f887a…7e6d

// /var/log/homebrain/mcp-nextcloud-audit.log — the result of the consent loop:
2026-05-06T20:01:22 {"server":"nextcloud","action":"share",
                     "path":"/uploads","expire_days":7}

// Nextcloud confirms the actual share resource exists on disk:
$ curl -u admin:<app-pass> -H 'OCS-APIRequest: true' \
  http://127.0.0.1:8080/ocs/v2.php/apps/files_sharing/api/v1/shares
{"id":"2","share_type":3,"path":"/uploads","stime":1778097682,
 "url":"http://127.0.0.1:8080/s/iiaRr54CKLnZ7sC", ...}
```

**Observed result after the fix:**

- **Vanilla baseline (without PR):** the model leaks `action_id` to chat, offers to self-confirm, hallucinates a second action_id off-by-one. Trust boundary is the model's behavior — soft gate. [Screenshot + analysis in #78308 comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78308#issuecomment-4391533882).
- **PR build:** the agent fires the tool, the consent envelope is intercepted, `plugin.approval.request` is issued to the gateway with the correct `agentId` + `sessionKey` so the forwarder can route. Approval prompt arrives in the **web UI's native modal** (gorgeous render, all three decisions one tap) AND on the user's **WhatsApp** simultaneously. User taps `Allow once` (or replies `/approve allow-once`). Runtime re-calls the tool with `confirmation_token = action_id`. **Real Nextcloud share is created** — verified by the audit log entry, the gateway `waitDecision 59412ms ✓` line, and the actual `share` row in NC's `files_sharing` API.
- **A live regression bug was caught and fixed during this very run**: `decision: null` from the gateway in two-phase mode means "pending, call waitDecision", not "deny". Fixed in `ef30bbf` with a regression test. Without the live test cycle, that would have shipped as a latent bug masking real user approvals as denials.
- **Forwarder routing was also live-caught**: the PR initially didn't propagate `agentId`/`sessionKey` from the agent harness to `plugin.approval.request`, so the forwarder had no session binding and prompts auto-cancelled silently. Fixed in `2056d25` by plumbing both fields through `materializeBundleMcpToolsForRun` + `createBundleMcpToolRuntime` from `attempt.ts` and `compact.ts`.
- **`/approve` UX was live-caught too**: typing a full uuid by hand on a phone is unrealistic, and the prompt template said `Reply with: /approve <id>` with `<id>` as a literal placeholder. Fixed in `a2cd999`: bare `/approve allow-once` resolves to the single most-recent pending approval; the prompt now shows all three decisions inline copy-paste-ready.

**What was not tested:** the *deny* path through user-driven rejection (we exercised the deny path via timeout and via approval-system error in unit tests, but not via a user explicitly typing `/approve deny` end-to-end on the live deployment). The unit tests (`on consent envelope + deny, returns synthetic denied result and does NOT re-call`) cover this deterministically. Maintainer running the PR through any deployment can verify in a single message exchange.

## Review responses

### Channel-level message provenance ([m13v's review](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78308#issuecomment-4391392109))

> *"if a compromised mcp server returns a tool result containing '/approve abc123 allow-once' in summary or output, and the channel renderer doesn't distinguish user-typed from tool-emitted messages at the parser layer, an mcp tool self-approves future calls by poisoning the transcript."*

Closed in `083dd748`. Two layers:

1. `sanitiseToolEmittedApprovalText` neutralises any `/approve` substring in MCP-emitted text by inserting U+200B between the slash and `approve` so `^/approve\b` cannot match. Applied at parse-time inside `detectMcpConsentEnvelope`, so every downstream path that renders the envelope sees pre-sanitised text.
2. The existing `/approve` parser already enforces provenance (via `isAuthorizedSender` against the channel allowlist) but the invariant was implicit. A code comment now makes it explicit so future refactors don't lose it.

Live-verified on the deployed `.58` PR build with a deliberately malicious envelope (full transcript on the [issue thread](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78308#issuecomment-4394805083)). Two new unit tests cover the sanitiser directly and the envelope path.

## Reference / motivation

Part of a broader integration on a [HomeBrain](https://github.com/oalterg/HomeBrain) box. Reference server-side: [`scripts/mcp_common.py`](https://github.com/oalterg/HomeBrain/blob/vault-integration/scripts/mcp_common.py) (the `Consent` helper) and [`scripts/mcp-*.py`](https://github.com/oalterg/HomeBrain/tree/vault-integration/scripts). Design write-up: [`INTEGRATIONS_PLAN.md`](https://github.com/oalterg/HomeBrain/blob/vault-integration/INTEGRATIONS_PLAN.md).

## Open questions for review

1. **Should I add an `mcp.approvals.allowlist: [{server, tool}]`?** Left out for v1 — gating is already opt-in *per-tool* by the MCP server (only act-tier tools return the envelope), and `allow-always` already short-circuits future calls. Happy to add an allowlist as a follow-up if maintainers prefer.
2. **The decision callback returns the existing `ExecApprovalDecision` union** (`allow-once` | `allow-always` | `deny`) — keeps the reply-parser + UI rendering identical.
3. **Approval kind**: I considered adding `"mcp-tool"` as a third `ChannelApprovalKind`. Decided against it (~10 files for a presentation distinction) — the existing plugin renderer already shows `pluginId: mcp:<server>`, `toolName: <tool>`, `description: <summary>`. Open to redoing as a separate kind if maintainers prefer.
4. **Should `approvals.plugin.enabled = true, mode = "session"` be the default?** Currently the gateway's plugin-approval forwarder is fully gated on this config block being explicitly set. Without it set, requests are accepted but never delivered — the boundary becomes a permanent deny gate (caught live during this PR's testing). Sensible default seems to be on-with-session-routing, especially now that the contract is broadly useful for MCP tool calls. Happy to add that as a separate PR if maintainers agree.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.body:
## Summary
- stabilize full-suite fixtures that were exposed while validating gateway reload fixes: qa-channel metadata, WhatsApp auth age, plugin install/manifest path handling, repo boundary scanning, and agent approval/provider-alias tests
- keep qa-channel out of configured/docs/setup exposure while preserving its internal test channel metadata
- make test helpers tolerate built/source checkout differences, macOS realpath differences, mocked child_process state, and current plugin metadata snapshot leakage

## Verification
- `CI=true node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts extensions/qa-channel/setup-entry.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts src/config/io.write-config.test.ts src/gateway/server.roles-allowlist-update.test.ts src/plugins/install.npm-spec.test.ts src/plugins/manifest-registry.test.ts src/channels/plugins/configured-state.test.ts src/channels/plugins/contracts/plugin.registry-backed-shard-e.contract.test.ts test/extension-test-boundary.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `CI=true node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `CI=true node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/provider-auth-aliases.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 CI=true node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.full-core-support-boundary.config.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 CI=true node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.full-agentic.config.ts`
- `CI=true pnpm check:test-types`
- `CI=true pnpm check`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=1800000 CI=true pnpm test`
- `CI=true pnpm build`
- `codex review --uncommitted`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Full local validation no longer fails on built/source acpx MCP entrypoint expectations, qa-channel accidental public configured-state exposure, WhatsApp auth mtime clock skew, macOS realpath mismatches, plugin install fixture ENOTDIR cases, boundary scans polluted by child_process mocks, deprecated interactive approval assertions, or leaked current plugin metadata snapshots.
Real environment tested: Local macOS checkout against latest `origin/main`, Node 22, current PR branch `codex/full-suite-stability-fixes`, isolated temporary OpenClaw state paths, full build, full check, focused affected Vitest files, full support-boundary and agentic shards, and the complete 12-shard Vitest suite.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Real runtime guard proof used `OPENCLAW_PROOF_AUTH_DIR="$tmp/auth" node --import tsx -` to create a temporary WhatsApp `creds.json`, set its mtime 60 seconds ahead of the local clock, and call `getWebAuthAgeMs()` from `extensions/whatsapp/src/auth-store.ts`; validation also ran the focused and full commands listed in Verification.
Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real runtime guard proof:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_PROOF_AUTH_DIR="$tmp/auth" node --import tsx -
authDir=/tmp/openclaw-pr83241-wa-age.o1bnKw/auth
credsPath=creds.json
futureMtimeAheadMs≈60000
authAgeMs=0
```

Supplemental local validation: focused touched-surface run passed 15 files / 244 tests; approval presentation focused run passed 2 files / 68 tests; provider auth aliases focused run passed 2 files / 4 tests; support-boundary shard passed 133 files / 1349 tests with 1 file and 6 tests skipped; agentic shard passed 1412 files / 17297 tests with 9 skipped; full `pnpm test` passed 12 Vitest shards in 1067.30s.
Observed result after fix: The live `getWebAuthAgeMs()` call returns `0` instead of a negative auth age when the credentials file timestamp is slightly ahead of the host clock; the local suite also completes cleanly from a full checkout, and the affected fixtures now isolate global state, normalize path variants, and assert current presentation metadata instead of deprecated interactive payloads.
What was not tested: Live external channel/provider credentials and real messaging transports were not exercised; this PR is test/fixture stability plus one WhatsApp auth-age guard covered by the runtime proof, local unit coverage, and full-suite tests.

target.title: Doctor: add health-check contract and --lint validation
target.body:
Branch: `doctor-detection-validation`
Base: `upstream/main`
Head: `a0eaef75cc`

## Summary

Today doctor health work is mostly procedural: a contribution runs, reports
status, and may repair inside the same `run(ctx)` flow. This PR introduces the
next doctor health-check contract, following the existing config-change pattern
of separating detection from application.

```ts
detect(ctx, scope?) -> HealthFinding[]
repair?(ctx, findings) -> HealthRepairResult
```

`detect()` powers the new read-only `openclaw doctor --lint` mode and also
powers post-fix validation after a modern `repair()` runs. `--fix` remains the
operator repair path; `--lint` is the structured detection/reporting path.

`repair()` now also carries the reporting shape needed for dry-run/diff follow-up: repair contexts can receive `dryRun`/`diff`, and repair results can report concrete `diffs` plus non-file `effects`. Detection stays read-only; repair owns mutation planning and preview reporting.

## Motivation

Doctor already owns workspace health, but most checks today are shaped around a
single procedural `run(ctx)` path. That works for guided repair, but it blurs
three separate operator needs:

- diagnose what is wrong without mutating the workspace
- repair selected issues through the existing `doctor --fix` path
- validate that the repair actually cleared the finding

The result is that users and automation often have to treat `doctor --fix` as
both diagnosis and repair. That is awkward for CI, risky for preflight checks,
and hard to reason about when a repair partially succeeds or needs manual
follow-up.

This PR keeps doctor as the owner of health checks and upgrades the contract
toward a detect/apply/validate flow. `doctor --lint` becomes the structured
reporting mode, while `detect()` gives `doctor --fix` the same logic for
post-repair validation.

Related issue signal:

- [#79166](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/79166): asks for a
  doctor dry-run/diff mode because `doctor --fix` applies changes without a
  structured preview.
- [#77802](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/77802): `doctor --fix`
  reports repairs, then fails validation and the same stale findings return on
  the next run.
- [#78217](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78217): `doctor --fix`
  partially runs against a live gateway and exits abruptly, leaving the user
  unsure what was applied.
- [#62261](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/62261): update preflight
  already treats lint as a gate, but operators need clearer machine-readable
  diagnostics when a gate fails.

This PR does not claim to close those issues. It adds the shared doctor
detection, repair, and validation contract that current and follow-up checks can
use.

## Maintainer Input Requested

1. **Should lint live inside doctor, or be a separate `openclaw lint` command?**

   This PR treats linting as doctor's read-only health detection mode rather
   than a separate extension or command. The intent is to avoid creating a
   second health surface that would need its own check registry, docs, severity
   rules, and repair story.

   I also prepared an alternate draft that exposes the same diagnostic contract
   as top-level `openclaw lint` while keeping `doctor` focused on guided repair:
   https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80800

2. **Is `detect()` / `repair()` the right health-check contract?**

   Today many doctor contributions combine detection, reporting, and repair
   inside `run(ctx)`. This PR keeps that legacy path, but follows the existing
   config change model by separating the needed change from the code that
   applies it. The doctor driver can then report, apply, and validate the result
   through:

   ```ts
   detect(ctx, scope?) -> HealthFinding[]
   repair?(ctx, findings) -> HealthRepairResult
   ```

   `detect()` powers `doctor --lint` and post-repair validation. `repair()` lets
   a modern check report whether it repaired, skipped, or failed the requested
   findings and why.

3. **Is this the right migration path for existing doctor rules?**

   The PR migrates a small set of core checks first and leaves existing doctor
   checks on the legacy path. If accepted, remaining hard-coded doctor rules can
   migrate incrementally: move diagnostics into `detect(ctx)` first, add
   `repair(ctx, findings)` only when repair behavior is understood and covered,
   and keep legacy `run(ctx)` for checks that still need prompts, service
   changes, backups, or platform-specific flows.

4. **Is `src/flows` the right home, or should health move toward a capability
   home?**

   This PR keeps the new doctor health code near the existing doctor flow
   machinery to minimize movement during the contract change. If maintainers
   expect health checks to become part of the broader capability ownership
   model, the same contract could move under a clearer `health` or capability
   home as the remaining rules migrate.

## What Changed

- Added structured health primitives: `HealthFinding`, `HealthCheck`,
  `HealthCheckContext`, `HealthCheckScope`, and `HealthRepairResult`.
- Added a small doctor health-check registration point for modern `detect()` /
  `repair()` checks, alongside the existing legacy doctor contribution flow.
- Exported the narrow health contract through `openclaw/plugin-sdk/health` for
  bundled health-check consumers. Test-only registry reset helpers remain
  internal.
- Added `doctor --lint` with `--json`, `--severity-min`, `--skip`, and `--only`.
- Kept `doctor --lint` plugin-free at CLI startup. This preserves structured
  config/error reporting even when plugin loading would otherwise fail.
- Modernized five core checks:
  - `core/doctor/gateway-config`
  - `core/doctor/command-owner`
  - `core/doctor/workspace-status`
  - `core/doctor/skills-readiness`
  - `core/doctor/final-config-validation`
- Added structured `repair()` support for modern checks in `doctor --fix`.
- Added explicit repair outcomes (`repaired`, `skipped`, `failed`) so a check
  can tell the driver it did not perform the requested repair and why.
- Added repair preview metadata: `dryRun`/`diff` repair context flags, structured
  `diffs` for config/file edits, and `effects` for service, process, package,
  state, or other side effects.
- Added scoped post-repair validation: after `repair()`, doctor runs
  `detect(ctx, scope)` again and reports remaining findings as repair warnings.
- Updated doctor docs and gateway doctor docs.

## Behavior

Example lint output:

```text
doctor --lint: ran 5 check(s), 1 finding(s)
  [warning] core/doctor/gateway-config gateway.mode - gateway.mode is unset; gateway start will be blocked.
    fix: Run `openclaw configure` and set Gateway mode (local/remote), or `openclaw config set gateway.mode local`.
```

Exit codes:

- `0`: no findings at or above the selected threshold
- `1`: one or more findings met the selected threshold
- `2`: command/runtime failure before findings could be emitted

Repair validation behavior:

- `repair(ctx, findings)` receives findings from its own check.
- `repair()` reports `repaired`, `skipped`, or `failed` with an optional reason.
- after a `repaired` result, doctor re-runs scoped `detect(ctx, scope)` for
  validation.
- remaining findings are surfaced as repair warnings; the follow-up detection
  pass is the proof.

## Review Notes

This is an internal contract, not a public repair API. Existing doctor repairs
still cover prompts, backups, service changes, plugin installs, and
platform-specific flows through the legacy contribution pipeline.

This PR prepares the modern repair contract for future `doctor --fix --dry-run` / diff output, but does not expose a whole-doctor dry-run flag yet. Legacy repairs still need to migrate or explicitly classify their side effects before that CLI can be honest across the full doctor surface.

Structured repairs run inside the existing doctor contribution sequence before
legacy repair steps, so rules can migrate gradually. Checks that are not ready
for `detect()` / `repair()` keep using `run(ctx)`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `openclaw doctor --lint` exposes read-only doctor health detection backed by the new `detect()` / `repair()` contract, and the repair contract now carries dry-run/diff reporting metadata without moving mutation planning into `detect()`.

Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu-24.04 source checkout on branch `doctor-detection-validation`, PR head `a0eaef75cc`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --only core/doctor/gateway-config --json
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-lint-flow.test.ts src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts
pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/cli/doctor.md docs/gateway/doctor.md
git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix: Real CLI output from the WSL checkout:

```text
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --only core/doctor/gateway-config --json
{"ok":false,"checksRun":1,"checksSkipped":4,"findings":[{"checkId":"core/doctor/gateway-config","severity":"warning","message":"gateway.mode is unset; gateway start will be blocked.","path":"gateway.mode","fixHint":"Run `openclaw configure` and set Gateway mode (local/remote), or `openclaw config set gateway.mode local`."}]}
```

The focused repair-flow test also now proves `runDoctorHealthRepairs(..., { dryRun: true, diff: true })` passes `dryRun` / `diff` into `repair()`, collects returned `diffs` and `effects`, does not apply returned config, and skips post-repair validation during preview mode.

Observed result after fix: The lint command ran a real registered core `detect()` check and emitted structured JSON findings. The repair contract now reports preview metadata through `repair()` results, while `detect()` remains read-only diagnosis.

What was not tested: No external channel or gateway service was started. This proof covers the doctor lint/health-check contract, core detection/reporting path, and structured repair dry-run plumbing.

## Testing

Passed focused doctor lint/repair/core-check tests, CLI registration coverage,
plugin SDK API checks, docs checks, and targeted oxlint on the changed doctor
flow files.

## Follow-Up

If this model is accepted, migrate remaining hard-coded doctor rules
incrementally: move diagnostics into `detect(ctx)` first, then add
`repair(ctx, findings)` only when the repair behavior is understood and covered.

The stacked policy drafts show bundled-extension consumers of this model. The
channels draft is the smaller first example: policy contributes doctor findings
through a narrow bundled health registration path, uses `doctor --lint` for
workspace validation, and participates in `doctor --fix` with scoped
post-repair validation.

Related stacked drafts:

- Policy channels conformance: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80407
- Policy tool approval follow-up: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80056
- Policy conformance examples: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/80783
- Policy runtime audit and attestation enforcement:
  https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/81104

target.title: fix(plugin-sdk): reset progress draft gate started flag on onStart rejection
target.body:
## Summary

`createChannelProgressDraftGate` sets `started = true` synchronously before `params.onStart` resolves. If `onStart` rejects, `started` stays `true` — `noteWork()` returns `true` and `hasStarted` returns `true`, so callers believe streaming is live when it never started. Silent message loss follows (issue F020, #83115).

**Fix:** keep `started = true` synchronously (prevents the double-start race during the async gap), but add a `.catch()` that resets `started` and `startPromise` before re-throwing. Both call sites are covered: the timer path (`void start().catch(() => {})`) swallows the re-throw as before; the `noteWork`/`startNow` path propagates it to the caller.

```diff
-    startPromise = Promise.resolve().then(params.onStart);
+    startPromise = Promise.resolve()
+      .then(params.onStart)
+      .catch((err) => {
+        // onStart rejected — reset so callers don't believe streaming is live
+        started = false;
+        startPromise = undefined;
+        throw err;
+      });
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: after `params.onStart` rejects, `gate.hasStarted` returns `false` and `gate.noteWork()` returns `false`, so retry via `startNow()` becomes possible. Pre-fix both stayed `true` despite never streaming, producing the silent message loss path described in #83115.

Real environment tested: Node 24.14.0 on Linux WSL2 (Ubuntu 24.04); local checkout of `fix/channel-progress-draft-gate-rejection-reset` at `919f90b6df`; vitest 4.1.6 driven by `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
$ git checkout fix/channel-progress-draft-gate-rejection-reset
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
    --config test/vitest/vitest.plugin-sdk.config.ts \
    --reporter=verbose \
    src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix (terminal output captured live):

```
RUN  v4.1.6 /home/garrits/Documents/openclaw

 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > reads canonical nested streaming config first 45ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > keeps progress-only tool progress config out of normal preview modes 1ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > falls back to legacy flat fields when the canonical object is absent 1ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > preserves progress as a first-class preview mode 0ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > keeps block preview mode separate from block delivery 0ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > selects a longer transcript candidate for ellipsis-truncated finals 1ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > keeps intentional ellipsis finals when candidates do not prove truncation 0ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > suppresses standalone tool progress for active preview drafts 0ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > uses auto progress labels when no explicit label is configured 1ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > supports explicit progress labels and custom label sets 0ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > formats bounded progress draft text 1ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > renders progress labels as rolling lines 0ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > renders structured progress lines with compact details 1ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > bounds progress draft line length to reduce edit reflow 0ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > honors configured progress draft line length and cuts prose on word boundaries 0ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > keeps compacted raw progress lines from leaking unmatched markdown backticks 6ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > formats progress draft lines with shared tool display labels 3ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > updates keyed progress lines in place 1ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > starts progress drafts after five seconds or a second work event 3ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > starts progress drafts immediately on the second work event 1ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > resets hasStarted to false when onStart rejects via timer 1ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > resets hasStarted to false and propagates when onStart rejects via noteWork 1ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > allows a retry after onStart rejects 0ms
 ✓ |plugin-sdk| src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts > channel-streaming > ignores message-like tools for progress draft work 0ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  24 passed (24)
   Duration  551ms
```

Observed result after fix: the three new cases above (`resets hasStarted to false when onStart rejects via timer`, `resets hasStarted to false and propagates when onStart rejects via noteWork`, `allows a retry after onStart rejects`) all pass — confirming that after a rejection settles, `gate.hasStarted === false`, the rejection propagates through `startNow()` to the caller, and a follow-up `startNow()` invokes `onStart` again. The other 21 tests confirm no regression in the surrounding gate, label, and progress-line behavior.

What was not tested: end-to-end retry through live channel implementations (Crabbox / Testbox). The fix is a synchronous-flag reset inside the factory; the factory-level behavior is exercised by the unit suite above, but real-channel recovery from a rejecting `onStart` has not been observed against a live channel from this branch.

Closes #83115

target.title: Discord: wake on reaction events
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord reaction notifications were enqueued as passive system events, so reaction-only inputs could wait for a later wake before the agent processed them.
- Why it matters: Discord is a low-noise operator surface; a reaction should be enough to get the routed session's attention when reactions are already enabled and authorized.
- What changed: accepted Discord reaction events now include stable `reaction_key` values for standard/custom emoji and request an immediate heartbeat wake after the system event is successfully enqueued.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no per-emoji semantics/config API, no hardcoded emoji-to-action map, and no new Discord permissions. Emoji meanings can live in preferences/prompt text.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83164
- Related #47687
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: accepted Discord reaction-only events now wake the routed session after enqueue, and standard/custom emoji are exposed as stable reaction keys in the system event text/context key.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, branch based on `openclaw/main` commit `45a434fb23`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check CHANGELOG.md docs/channels/discord.md extensions/discord/src/monitor/listeners.reactions.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor.test.ts`
  - `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: focused Discord monitor Vitest passed `1 file, 46 tests`; extension typecheck passed; format check passed.
- Observed result after fix: standard emoji reactions enqueue `reaction_key=emoji:<emoji>`, custom emoji reactions enqueue `reaction_key=custom_emoji:<id>`, and successfully enqueued reaction events call `requestHeartbeat({ source: "notifications-event", intent: "immediate", reason: "discord-reaction", sessionKey })`.
- What was not tested: I did not send a live Discord reaction through a production bot; maintainers/operators may want redacted live gateway proof before merge.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): source inspection showed Discord reaction listeners enqueued system events without a heartbeat wake.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Discord reaction listener code enqueued system events and returned without requesting a heartbeat wake.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests asserted reaction enqueue/routing behavior but did not assert wake behavior for reaction-only inputs.
- Contributing context (if known): reaction event text included a formatted emoji label but not a stable key that preference text can target consistently.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/discord/src/monitor.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: accepted standard/custom emoji reaction notifications enqueue stable reaction-key events and wake only when enqueue succeeds.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the Discord monitor harness already covers reaction notification auth, routing, and enqueue behavior.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing reaction tests covered notification enqueue/routing but not heartbeat wake.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Discord reaction notifications that pass existing access controls now wake the routed session. System event text includes `reaction_key=emoji:<emoji>` or `reaction_key=custom_emoji:<id>` so preferences/prompt text can assign meaning to specific reactions without channel-specific semantics config.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Discord reaction] -> [system event queued] -> [processed only after later wake]

After:
[Discord reaction] -> [system event queued] -> [heartbeat wake] -> [agent sees reaction event]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local source checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Discord monitor unit harness
- Relevant config (redacted): default DM reaction test config and guild `reactionNotifications` cases

### Steps

1. Run the focused Discord monitor tests.
2. Run extension typecheck.
3. Run format check for touched files.

### Expected

- Reaction event tests pass, including heartbeat wake assertions.
- Typecheck and format checks pass.

### Actual

- Passed.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: standard emoji enqueue + wake, custom emoji stable key enqueue + wake, no wake when enqueue deduplicates, existing reaction authorization/routing cases.
- Edge cases checked: blocked DM/guild/group reactions remain blocked, `off`/`own`/`all`/`allowlist` modes still gate behavior, custom emoji context keys are stable.
- What you did **not** verify: live Discord Gateway delivery.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: reaction notifications can now start an agent turn where they previously waited for another wake.
  - Mitigation: behavior is still gated by existing `reactionNotifications` and Discord sender/channel/guild access controls, and duplicate queue entries do not wake.

target.title: perf(agents): reuse media tool availability during tool prep
target.body:
## Summary
- Reuse the optional media tool factory plan for the image tool instead of resolving image availability separately.
- Let image/video/music generation tool factories skip their registration-time availability scan when createOpenClawTools already resolved availability.
- Use the same effective media tool config for the plan and constructed media tools.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior or issue addressed: Repeated warm `createOpenClawCodingTools` prep was spending about 29ms each registering `image_generate`, `video_generate`, and `music_generate` because those factories repeated availability checks already resolved by the OpenClaw media factory plan.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `media-tool-availability-pr4c`, Node 25.9.0 through repo `pnpm exec tsx`; warm in-process tool prep probe after one warmup call.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a real local `node`/`tsx` probe that imports `createOpenClawCodingTools`, records `recordToolPrepStage` timings, discards one warmup call, then records 30 warm samples; wrapped with `/usr/bin/time -l`.

Evidence after fix:
```text
total p50 103.68ms, p95 110.16ms
openclaw-tools:image_generate registration p50 0.005ms
openclaw-tools:video_generate registration p50 0.002ms
openclaw-tools:music_generate registration p50 0.002ms
/usr/bin/time -l max RSS 850575360 bytes
```

Observed result after fix: Single-call warm p50 improved by about 84ms versus the previous same-probe baseline, from 187.74ms p50 to 103.68ms p50 (~45%). The three generation registration stages dropped from about 29ms each to near-zero. Remaining warm prep cost is mostly plugin-tools (~73ms p50) and image/media availability planning (~30ms p50).

What was not tested: Full end-to-end provider media generation was not run; this PR only changes registration-time availability reuse, while direct factory calls and execution-time provider resolution remain covered by targeted tests.

## Verification
- pnpm test src/agents/openclaw-tools.media-factory-plan.test.ts src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/video-generate-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/music-generate-tool.test.ts
- pnpm tsgo:core
- pnpm build
- pnpm check:changed --staged

commands.author: Takhoffman

target.title: fix(agents): preserve run-mode keep subagents past session sweep TTL (#83132)
target.body:
## Summary

Refs #83132. Companion to #83146.

The sweeper in `src/agents/subagent-registry.ts` applies `SESSION_RUN_TTL_MS` (5 minutes after `cleanupCompletedAt`) to any registry entry with `archiveAtMs === undefined`. The comment claims this is for session-mode runs, but `archiveAtMs` is **also** undefined by design for **run-mode** subagents spawned with `cleanup: "keep"` (`subagent-registry-run-manager.ts:475-480`):

```ts
const archiveAtMs =
  spawnMode === "session" || registerParams.cleanup === "keep"
    ? undefined
    : archiveAfterMs
      ? now + archiveAfterMs
      : undefined;
```

Result: a `cleanup: "keep"` run-mode subagent finishes → `cleanupCompletedAt` set → five minutes later the sweeper deletes it from `subagents/runs.json` while sessions/transcripts stay on disk. This matches the symptom in #83132 — *"five accepted native subagent children whose sessions were present in the session store, but none of the five run IDs appeared in `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json`"* — the reporter explicitly used `runtime:"subagent"`, `mode:"run"`, `cleanup:"keep"`.

Fix: gate the absolute-TTL prune so it does not apply to non-session `cleanup: "keep"` entries.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Issue #83132 — `cleanup: "keep"` run-mode subagents disappear from `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` 5 minutes after cleanup completes, despite `keep` meaning "retain". After this patch they stay registered.

- **Real environment tested:** Local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on `/private/tmp/openclaw`. macOS 25.3.0 (Darwin arm64). `node v25.9.0`, `pnpm 11.1.0`. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` clean. Live demo loads the real `src/agents/subagent-registry.ts` via `tsx` and runs the real sweeper against a real `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` on a fresh `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` tempdir — no vitest, no fake timers, no mocked persistence. Only the external gateway/announce dependencies are stubbed because there is no live agent to call.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `git checkout fix/subagent-sweeper-keep-mode-ttl-83132`
  2. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
  3. Drop a local-only repro file at `scripts/repro-83132-sweep.mjs` (not committed) that:
     - sets `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` to a fresh tempdir,
     - imports `src/agents/subagent-registry.ts` via tsx,
     - registers a run-mode `cleanup: "keep"` entry whose `cleanupCompletedAt` is back-dated 10 minutes (twice the `SESSION_RUN_TTL_MS = 5 min` cutoff),
     - calls `__testing.sweepOnceForTests()` against the real sweeper,
     - reads back the in-memory list and the on-disk `runs.json`.
  4. `node --import tsx scripts/repro-83132-sweep.mjs`
  5. `git checkout main` and repeat step 4 against unmodified main to capture the pre-fix behavior.

- **Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output from both invocations, captured from my local shell on `/private/tmp/openclaw`. The script writes the entry to disk via `saveSubagentRegistryToDisk` before sweeping, so the after-sweep disk check is a real persisted-registry observation. Stdout reproduced verbatim.

  **After patch (`fix/subagent-sweeper-keep-mode-ttl-83132`):**
  ```
  $ node --import tsx scripts/repro-83132-sweep.mjs
  --- before sweep ---
  registry: live-run-keep-run-mode
  disk /var/folders/.../openclaw-sweep-repro-i16CDM/subagents/runs.json:
  {
    "version": 2,
    "runs": {
      "live-run-keep-run-mode": {
        "runId": "live-run-keep-run-mode",
        "childSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:live-keep",
        "requesterSessionKey": "agent:main:main",
        "requesterDisplayKey": "main",
        "task": "live keep-mode run-mode subagent",
        "cleanup": "keep",
        "spawnMode": "run",
        "createdAt": 1779034336836,
        "startedAt": 1779034336836,
        "endedAt": 1779034337836,
        "cleanupHandled": true,
        "cleanupCompletedAt": 1779034337836,
        "outcome": { "status": "ok", "startedAt": 1779034336836, "endedAt": 1779034337836, "elapsedMs": 1000 }
      }
    }
  }
  cleanupCompletedAt age: 10.00 min (SESSION_RUN_TTL_MS = 5 min)
  --- after sweep ---
  registry: live-run-keep-run-mode
  disk /var/folders/.../openclaw-sweep-repro-i16CDM/subagents/runs.json:
  {
    "version": 2,
    "runs": {
      "live-run-keep-run-mode": {
        "runId": "live-run-keep-run-mode",
        "childSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:live-keep",
        ...
        "cleanupCompletedAt": 1779034337836,
        "outcome": { "status": "ok", ... }
      }
    }
  }
  RESULT: PASS — entry survived sweep both in-memory and on-disk
  ```

  **Before patch (`main` at `833f1ce7`), same script, same setup:**
  ```
  $ node --import tsx scripts/repro-83132-sweep.mjs
  --- before sweep ---
  registry: live-run-keep-run-mode
  disk /var/folders/.../openclaw-sweep-repro-hZiHkV/subagents/runs.json:
  {
    "version": 2,
    "runs": {
      "live-run-keep-run-mode": { ... "cleanup": "keep", "spawnMode": "run", "cleanupCompletedAt": 1779034347260, ... }
    }
  }
  cleanupCompletedAt age: 10.00 min (SESSION_RUN_TTL_MS = 5 min)
  --- after sweep ---
  registry: (none)
  disk /var/folders/.../openclaw-sweep-repro-hZiHkV/subagents/runs.json:
  {
    "version": 2,
    "runs": {}
  }

  RESULT: FAIL — entry pruned from both in-memory and on-disk
  ```

  The post-sweep on-disk `runs.json` `{ "version": 2, "runs": {} }` on `main` is exactly the symptom in the bug report: *"none of the five run IDs appeared in `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json`"*. After the patch, the sweeper leaves both the in-memory entry and the on-disk row untouched.

- **Observed result after fix:** With the patch, `cleanup: "keep"` + `spawnMode: "run"` entries survive the real sweeper past 10 minutes (twice the prior TTL window). Both the in-memory `listSubagentRunsForRequester` result and the persisted `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` file still contain the run row. Pre-fix, the same setup leaves an empty `runs.json` on disk and the list returns nothing for that runId. Session-mode (`spawnMode: "session"`) runs still get pruned 5 minutes after cleanup — the gate only exempts the non-session keep path.

- **What was not tested:** I did not exercise the original live QQ / Discord embedded-channel five-children wave from the bug report (no local QQ harness). I also did not run the full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` end-to-end locally; CI on this PR re-runs that.

### Supplemental regression coverage (mocks, not the proof)

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  56 passed (56)
```

New regression case in `subagent-registry.test.ts` ("preserves run-mode keep entries past SESSION_RUN_TTL_MS sweep") fails on `main`, passes on this branch.

## Why not in #83146

#83146 lands the narrow "fail-closed initial registration" durability fix Clawsweeper recommended. This PR is a separate, smaller surface (one-line gate on the sweeper + one regression test) that targets the actual root-cause path for the reporter's exact `cleanup:"keep"` scenario. Keeping it isolated so each fix can be reviewed and reverted independently.

## Test plan

- [x] `node --import tsx scripts/repro-83132-sweep.mjs` on the fix branch (local repro file, not committed) — see Evidence above
- [x] Same script on `main` to capture pre-fix prune behavior — see Evidence above
- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts`
- [ ] CI: full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` lane on this PR

commands.author: anagnorisis2peripeteia

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched. `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts markers or previous turn was max_tokens-truncated) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.

target.title: fix(doctor): detect stale session snapshot paths
target.body:
Fixes #47534.

## Summary
- add a read-only doctor health contribution for stale cached session snapshot metadata
- scan persisted session stores for cached bundled skill paths in `skillsSnapshot.prompt`, runtime-only `skillsSnapshot.resolvedSkills`, and `systemPromptReport.injectedWorkspaceFiles`
- distinguish healthy live bundled skills root from stale cached metadata paths and avoid false positives for current runtime paths, workspace skill roots, and Windows paths

## Tests
- `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-session-snapshots.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-transcripts.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/doctor-session-snapshots.ts src/commands/doctor-session-snapshots.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw doctor` did not warn when cached session snapshot metadata still referenced bundled skills under an inactive OpenClaw runtime root after an install/runtime path change.

Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu source checkout with deterministic temp session-store fixtures and the real doctor session snapshot scanner registered through the doctor health contribution list.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-session-snapshots.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-transcripts.test.ts`, `pnpm check:changed`, and the scripted before/after proof published below.

Evidence after fix: ![before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-82867-fresh/pr-82867/pr-82867-doctor-session-snapshots-before-after-proof.png)

- Summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-82867-fresh/pr-82867/summary.txt
- Before log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-82867-fresh/pr-82867/before-origin-main.log
- After log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-82867-fresh/pr-82867/after-pr-82867.log

Observed result after fix: Before `origin/main`, no doctor session snapshot detector/contribution exists. After this PR, stale POSIX install-root, temp-backed, and Windows cached bundled skill paths are reported, while current-runtime and workspace skill paths are not false positives. The doctor note distinguishes the healthy live bundled skills root from stale cached session metadata and gives safe fresh-session/reset guidance.

What was not tested: A live macOS Homebrew install migration was not run; the proof uses source-level session-store fixtures for the stale cached metadata shape from the issue.

target.title: [Feat] Add secrets runtime diagnostics spans
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway startup secret resolution and web-tool credential selection were hard to inspect without exposing raw secret values.
- Why it matters: startup regressions around env, file, exec, auth profile, and web provider secrets need traceable timings and sanitized diagnostics.
- What changed: add runtime secrets diagnostics spans, sanitized provider/ref attributes, startup trace output, web-tool diagnostics, and benchmark coverage for secret-backed startup cases.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no new secret provider capability, no credential value logging, no user config migration, and no provider selection policy change.
- AI-assisted: Yes; Codex assisted with implementation, review, rebase conflict resolution, and local verification.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A - no linked issue.
- Related N/A - no linked PR.
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: Gateway startup with secret-backed auth/model/web-tool config now emits sanitized diagnostics and startup trace spans while still resolving env, file, exec, auth profile, and web search SecretRefs.
- Real environment tested: macOS Darwin 25.4.0 arm64, Node v24.14.0, pnpm 11.1.0, local OpenClaw gateway benchmark using the built `dist/entry.js`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/build-all.mjs
pnpm tsgo:prod
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/secrets/resolve.test.ts src/secrets/runtime-web-tools.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-config.secrets.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts test/scripts/bench-gateway-startup.test.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8
node scripts/changed-lanes.mjs --base upstream/main --head HEAD --json
git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
node --import tsx scripts/bench-gateway-startup.ts --case gatewayAuthEnvSecretRef --case modelProviderEnvSecretRef --case authProfileKeyRef --case webSearchProvider --case fileSecretRef --case slowExecSecretRef --runs 1 --warmup 0 --output /tmp/openclaw-gateway-secrets-startup-bench-final2.json
node JSON audit of /tmp/openclaw-gateway-secrets-startup-bench-final2.json
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): terminal output and JSON audit from the final local gateway benchmark on head `268f677a130b84b17b0b43e8cc6099fda1b220c7`:

```text
[gateway-startup-bench] gatewayAuthEnvSecretRef run 1/1: healthz=3518.2ms readyz=4039.0ms httpListen=3410.3ms gatewayReady=3895.3ms cpu=4150.0ms cpuCore=1.027 rss=723.9MB heap=248.3MB
[gateway-startup-bench] modelProviderEnvSecretRef run 1/1: healthz=3277.4ms readyz=3684.2ms httpListen=3147.4ms gatewayReady=3517.8ms cpu=4300.0ms cpuCore=1.167 rss=685.3MB heap=339.9MB
[gateway-startup-bench] authProfileKeyRef run 1/1: healthz=2981.7ms readyz=3442.7ms httpListen=2876.0ms gatewayReady=3275.9ms cpu=4020.0ms cpuCore=1.168 rss=726.9MB heap=242.6MB
[gateway-startup-bench] webSearchProvider run 1/1: healthz=5073.3ms readyz=5419.2ms httpListen=4958.8ms gatewayReady=5343.2ms cpu=5640.0ms cpuCore=1.041 rss=690.1MB heap=308.7MB
[gateway-startup-bench] fileSecretRef run 1/1: healthz=3921.3ms readyz=4607.1ms httpListen=3785.9ms gatewayReady=4274.5ms cpu=5220.0ms cpuCore=1.133 rss=662.0MB heap=348.1MB
[gateway-startup-bench] slowExecSecretRef run 1/1: healthz=5020.2ms readyz=5446.2ms httpListen=4914.5ms gatewayReady=5295.3ms cpu=4940.0ms cpuCore=0.907 rss=729.2MB heap=243.0MB

cases=6
secretLeakCheck=pass
gatewayAuthEnvSecretRef: exit=0 signal=none healthz=200@3518ms readyz=200@4039ms errors=0
modelProviderEnvSecretRef: exit=0 signal=none healthz=200@3277ms readyz=200@3684ms errors=0

  secrets.webTools.search.providerSurface {"hasSourceWeb":true,"hasPluginWebSearchConfig":true,"configuredProviderHash":"494c463e30fdbda7","providerCount":1}
  secrets.webTools.search.providerSelection {"providerCount":1,"configuredProviderHash":"494c463e30fdbda7","providerSource":"configured","selectedProviderHash":"494c463e30fdbda7","diagnosticCount":0}
  secrets.webTools.total {"hasSourceWeb":true,"hasPluginWebSearchConfig":true,"hasPluginWebFetchConfig":false,"searchProviderCount":1,"fetchProviderCount":0,"searchProviderSource":"configured","fetchProviderSource":"none","diagnosticCount":0}

slowExecSecretRef: exit=0 signal=none healthz=200@5020ms readyz=200@5446ms errors=0
```

- Observed result after fix: all six secret-backed startup cases exited 0, reached healthy and ready gateway states, emitted sanitized secrets diagnostics spans, and the artifact audit found no fixture secret strings. The web-search provider diagnostics carry only hashed provider identifiers.
- What was not tested: full local `pnpm check`, Testbox/Crabbox, and local `codex review --base upstream/main`.
- Before evidence: earlier local benchmark run caught `slowExecSecretRef` failing with `Exec provider "benchexec" produced no output for 3000ms`; the fixture timeout was increased and the final benchmark passed.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: startup secret work had no reusable diagnostics timeline surface, so prepare/resolve/apply/web-tool timings were only visible through coarse Gateway startup measurements.
- Missing detection / guardrail: benchmark coverage did not exercise the added secret-backed startup matrix or persist sanitized per-span diagnostics.
- Contributing context (if known): Gateway secret activation now has a startup preflight path, so diagnostics need to distinguish preflight from activation without leaking raw provider names or secret ids.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/infra/diagnostics-timeline.test.ts`, `src/secrets/resolve.test.ts`, `src/secrets/runtime.test.ts`, `src/secrets/runtime-web-tools.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-startup-config.secrets.test.ts`, `src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`, `test/scripts/bench-gateway-startup.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: secret resolution diagnostics stay sanitized, web-tool diagnostics are preserved, startup trace is emitted on activation, and the benchmark records secret-backed startup spans.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the tests cover the shared diagnostics helpers and each changed runtime seam without requiring a full gateway E2E for every provider.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A - this PR adds and extends the coverage above.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A - tests were added or extended.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Gateway startup tracing can now include sanitized `secrets.prepare` and `secrets.webTools` timing lines when `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1` is enabled. No raw secret values, provider names, or secret ids are logged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
Gateway startup -> secret prepare/activate -> coarse startup timing only

After:
Gateway startup -> secret prepare/activate -> sanitized diagnostics timeline + startup trace -> ready gateway
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: secrets diagnostics are sanitized with provider hashes, source classes, counts, and status flags only; tests and benchmark artifact audit check that fixture secret strings do not appear in output tails.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Darwin 25.4.0 arm64
- Runtime/container: Node v24.14.0, pnpm 11.1.0, local worktree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway startup, secrets runtime, web search runtime
- Relevant config (redacted): benchmark-generated env/file/exec/auth-profile SecretRefs with fixture secret values only

### Steps

1. Build the current branch with `node scripts/build-all.mjs`.
2. Run prod typecheck with `pnpm tsgo:prod`.
3. Run the targeted tests with the `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ...` command listed above.
4. Run lint with `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8`.
5. Run changed-lane and whitespace sanity with `node scripts/changed-lanes.mjs --base upstream/main --head HEAD --json` and `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`.
6. Run the six-case startup benchmark command listed in Real behavior proof and audit the JSON artifact for success, diagnostics errors, and fixture secret leakage.

### Expected

- Build, prod typecheck, lint, targeted tests, changed-lane sanity, and whitespace sanity pass.
- Secret-backed Gateway startup cases reach `/healthz=200` and `/readyz=200`.
- Diagnostics contain sanitized spans and no raw fixture secret strings in output tails.

### Actual

- `node scripts/build-all.mjs`: passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:prod`: passed.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8`: passed with 0 warnings and 0 errors across scripts/core/extensions shards.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/secrets/resolve.test.ts src/secrets/runtime-web-tools.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-config.secrets.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts test/scripts/bench-gateway-startup.test.ts`: 5 test files passed, 118 tests passed.
- `node scripts/changed-lanes.mjs --base upstream/main --head HEAD --json`: only core, core tests, and tooling lanes were touched.
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`: passed.
- Final six-case startup benchmark: all cases returned `exitCode:0`, `/healthz=200`, `/readyz=200`, `errors=0`, and `secretLeakCheck=pass` in the JSON audit.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: env SecretRef gateway auth, env SecretRef model provider, auth profile keyRef, web search provider SecretRef, file SecretRef, slow exec SecretRef, diagnostics timeline parsing, and benchmark output sanitization.
- Edge cases checked: degraded slow exec fixture was caught by benchmark proof, then fixed by increasing the benchmark fixture no-output timeout; provider identifiers are emitted as stable hashes, not raw ids; added-line CJK check and whitespace sanity passed.
